skill.name: ponyflash
skill.description: Generate AI images, videos, speech, and music with the PonyFlash SDK, then process local media with FFmpeg workflows for clipping, merging, transcoding, frames, and subtitles.
skill.summary: Create AI media with PonyFlash and prepare finished assets with FFmpeg.
skill.author: ponyflash

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a high raw score because this community skill documents and ships shell scripts, FFmpeg workflows, network font downloads, and API-key setup. Review confirmed real medium-risk behavior that matches the stated media-generation purpose, while many Ruby backtick, weak-crypto, dynamic-import, and Windows SAM findings are false positives from markdown or comments. No prompt-injection attempt, credential exfiltration, hidden persistence, or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Local FFmpeg Execution on User Media
medium_findings.description: The media workflow runs ffmpeg and ffprobe against user-selected input, subtitle, and output paths. This is expected for a media editing skill, but it can process untrusted media and write local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly invokes ffmpeg and ffprobe for its documented media operations. Arguments are passed as shell words rather than eval strings, so command injection evidence is limited.

medium_findings.title: Network Font Download and Local Font Cache
medium_findings.description: The subtitle font helper downloads Noto Sans CJK SC from hardcoded public URLs, or from an environment-controlled URL. It writes the font into a cache or temporary font directory for subtitle rendering.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and filesystem behavior is explicit in the shell script and comments. The URLs serve a legitimate font purpose, but the environment override increases supply-chain risk.

medium_findings.title: Cloud API Key and Account Calls
medium_findings.description: Cloud generation tasks require a PonyFlash API key and call PonyFlash account and generation APIs. This is core functionality, but users must understand that prompts, media, and credentials are used with a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill clearly instructs users to provide or export an API key and then uses PonyFlash SDK methods. I did not find evidence that the key is sent anywhere except the intended SDK client.

low_findings.title: Dependency Check Prints Local PATH
low_findings.description: The FFmpeg checker prints the operating system name and PATH during normal output. This can disclose local environment details in shared logs, but it does not write files or call the network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script prints platform, PATH, and tool paths when quiet mode is not set. This is useful diagnostics, but it is a real low-severity information disclosure concern.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Documentation
low_findings.description: Many reported Ruby backtick, dynamic import, weak-crypto, and Windows SAM hits are documentation or comment artifacts. Examples include fenced Python imports, markdown command examples, and the word SECURITY MANIFEST.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed these examples are markdown snippets or comments, not executed Ruby code or Windows SAM access. The same files contain real shell behavior, which is captured separately.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local FFmpeg Execution on User Media
dangerous_patterns.description: The media workflow runs ffmpeg and ffprobe against user-selected input, subtitle, and output paths. This is expected for a media editing skill, but it can process untrusted media and write local files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script directly invokes ffmpeg and ffprobe for its documented media operations. Arguments are passed as shell words rather than eval strings, so command injection evidence is limited.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Font Download and Local Font Cache
dangerous_patterns.description: The subtitle font helper downloads Noto Sans CJK SC from hardcoded public URLs, or from an environment-controlled URL. It writes the font into a cache or temporary font directory for subtitle rendering.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network and filesystem behavior is explicit in the shell script and comments. The URLs serve a legitimate font purpose, but the environment override increases supply-chain risk.

content.value_statement: Creators need one workflow for AI generation and final media preparation. PonyFlash combines cloud media models with local FFmpeg editing, subtitles, and export guidance.
actual_capabilities: Generate images, videos, speech audio, and music through the PonyFlash Python SDK.
List PonyFlash models and check account credit balance before generation work.
Upload or reference files for PonyFlash media generation workflows.
Trim, concatenate, transcode, extract audio, and capture frames with FFmpeg helper scripts.
Build adaptive ASS subtitles and burn subtitles into local video files.
Use creative playbooks for multi-step production planning and asset assembly.
limitations: Cloud generation requires a valid PonyFlash API key and sufficient account credits.
Local editing requires installed ffmpeg and ffprobe with the needed encoders and filters.
Font preparation may download files from public endpoints unless local fonts are provided.
The skill provides workflow guidance and scripts, but generation quality depends on model behavior.

use_cases.title: Produce Social Video Assets
use_cases.description: Generate visuals, narration, and music, then assemble clips and subtitles into a publishable video.

use_cases.title: Prototype Product Media
use_cases.description: Create image and video concepts for campaigns, then export variants in common delivery formats.

use_cases.title: Prepare Local Media Deliverables
use_cases.description: Trim, merge, transcode, extract audio, capture thumbnails, and burn subtitles with repeatable FFmpeg commands.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Single Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 16:9 PonyFlash image of [subject] in [style]. Use a concise prompt and tell me the expected credit use first.

prompt_templates.title: Create Speech Audio
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate speech from this script using an appropriate PonyFlash voice: [script]. Suggest tone, speed, and output format before running.

prompt_templates.title: Edit a Local Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PonyFlash local media workflow to trim [input file] from [start] for [duration], then export an MP4 to [output path].

prompt_templates.title: Plan an End-to-End Production
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a production plan for a [duration] [format] video about [topic]. Include generated visuals, voice, music, subtitles, and final FFmpeg export steps.

output_examples.input: Create a short promotional video with narration and subtitles.
output: A step-by-step production plan with required assets and model choices.
Generated media tasks for visuals, speech, and background music.
A final export workflow that checks FFmpeg and burns subtitles into the video.

output_examples.input: Trim a product demo and capture a thumbnail.
output: Dependency check results for ffmpeg and ffprobe.
A trimmed MP4 using the requested time range.
A thumbnail image captured at the selected timestamp.

output_examples.input: Generate a music bed for a calm tutorial.
output_examples.output: A PonyFlash music generation request with style, duration, instrumental setting, and credit guidance.

best_practices: Verify the PonyFlash API key and credit balance before starting cloud generation.
Run FFmpeg capability checks before local editing, especially for subtitle burn-in.
Stage intermediate files in a temporary task directory and keep only requested deliverables.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys into reusable documents, examples, or saved output files.
Do not overwrite existing media outputs unless the user explicitly approves replacement.
Do not assume subtitle burn-in works until the subtitles filter has been checked.

faq.question: Does this skill require a PonyFlash API key?
faq.answer: Yes for cloud generation. Local FFmpeg editing tasks do not require a PonyFlash API key.

faq.question: Can it edit existing local videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes FFmpeg workflows for trimming, concatenation, transcoding, audio extraction, frames, and subtitles.

faq.question: Can it generate speech and music?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents PonyFlash speech synthesis and music generation through the Python SDK.

faq.question: Does it download anything automatically?
faq.answer: Subtitle font helpers may download Noto Sans CJK SC unless a local font path is supplied.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with sensitive media?
faq.answer: Use caution. Cloud generation sends prompts and assets to PonyFlash, while local FFmpeg work stays on the machine.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing outputs?
faq.answer: Review model outputs, confirm licensing needs, verify subtitles, and inspect final media quality before distribution.
